docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This documentation covers the architecture, infrastructure, and operational workflows for the CTF platform.

It is intended for: - Team members managing infrastructure - Challenge authors - Contributors onboarding into the system

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• 🔧 Architecture Overview
  • 🚀 Deployment Process
  • 🛠️ Deploy CTF Runbook
  • ➕ Add Challenge

docs.title: Repositories
docs.text:
• ctf-infra → infrastructure setup and configuration
  • ctf-challenges → challenge implementations
  • ctf-docs → documentation (this repo)

docs.title: High-Level Flow
docs.text:
User → Traefik → Challenge Containers

All services are hosted on a single VPS and exposed via HTTP routing.

docs.title: Traefik (Reverse Proxy)
docs.text:
Traefik handles all incoming HTTP requests and routes them to the correct challenge container.

  • Uses Docker labels for routing
  • Central entry point for all traffic
  • Can be extended with rate limiting and middleware

docs.title: Challenge Containers
docs.text:
Each challenge runs inside its own Docker container.

  • Isolated environment per challenge
  • Exposes a service (web, TCP, etc.)
  • Routed via Traefik

docs.title: Portainer
docs.text:
Portainer is used for managing Docker containers and stacks.

  • Provides a UI for deployment and monitoring
  • Not the source of truth (manual operations currently)

docs.title: Uptime Kuma
docs.text:
Used for monitoring service availability.

  • Tracks uptime of challenge endpoints
  • Helps detect failures during events

docs.title: Networking Model
docs.text:
• All containers run on the same Docker host
  • Traefik connects to containers via Docker network
  • External traffic flows only through Traefik

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Deployment is manual (no CI/CD)
  • No centralized logging
  • Limited observability into container behavior

docs.title: Future Improvements (Planned)
docs.text:
• Infrastructure as Code (Docker Compose)
  • Centralized logging (Loki stack)
  • Metrics and monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana)
  • Automated deployments

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: All challenges must follow a standardized structure to ensure consistency, reproducibility, and ease of deployment.

docs.title: Repository Layout
docs.text:
Challenges are organized by year, then category, then difficulty:

ctf-challenges/
  templates/                  # copy-paste scaffolds — one per category
  <year>/                     # e.g. 2026/
    <category>/               # e.g. web/
      <difficulty>/           # e.g. easy/
        <challenge-name>/     # e.g. sqli-1/
          Dockerfile
          docker-compose.yml
          challenge.yaml
          src/
          solve/

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
ctf-challenges/
  2026/
    web/
      easy/
        sqli-1/
    pwn/
      medium/
        buffer-overflow-1/
  2027/
    crypto/
      hard/
        rsa-3/

docs.title: Valid Categories
docs.text: Category Description web Web application vulnerabilities pwn Binary exploitation / memory corruption crypto Cryptographic weaknesses forensics File, memory, and network analysis stego Steganography and hidden data rev Reverse engineering misc Challenges that don't fit elsewhere

docs.title: Valid Difficulties
docs.text: Difficulty Description easy Introductory — suitable for beginners medium Intermediate — requires some experience hard Advanced — significant skill required

docs.title: Per-Challenge Required Files
docs.text:
Every challenge directory must contain exactly these files and directories:

challenge-name/
  Dockerfile          # builds the challenge image
  docker-compose.yml  # defines how the challenge runs
  challenge.yaml      # metadata
  src/                # challenge source code
  solve/              # intended solution and exploit scripts

docs.title: Dockerfile
docs.text:
Defines how the challenge container is built.

Requirements:

  • Use a minimal base image (e.g. python:3.12-slim, node:20-alpine)
  • Pin all dependency versions for reproducibility
  • Expose exactly one port per challenge (default: 5000)
  • Copy only required files into the image
  • Challenges must be fully self-contained (no external runtime dependencies)

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
Defines how the challenge runs.

Requirements:

  • Must define the service clearly using the challenge name
  • Must include Traefik labels for production routing
  • Use restart: unless-stopped for all challenge services
  • Avoid hardcoding secrets in compose files
services:
  challenge-name:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.<challenge-name>.rule=Host(`<challenge-name>.<domain>`)"
    restart: unless-stopped

docs.title: challenge.yaml
docs.text:
Metadata file for the challenge.

name: sqli-1
category: web
difficulty: easy
author: your-name
description: "A short description of the challenge scenario."
flag: CTF{example_flag}
flag_env: FLAG          # do not change — override only if strictly required (heavily discouraged)
port: 5000
Field Required Description name yes Lowercase, hyphen-separated identifier category yes One of the valid categories listed above difficulty yes easy, medium, or hard author yes Name or handle of the challenge author description yes Short description of the challenge scenario flag yes The flag in CTF{...} format flag_env yes Environment variable name the challenge reads the flag from — must be FLAG in virtually all cases; overriding is heavily discouraged port yes Port the service listens on inside the container

docs.title: src/
docs.text: Contains all challenge source code — everything needed to build and run the challenge service. The Dockerfile should copy from this directory.

docs.title: solve/
docs.text:
Contains:

  • A written explanation of the vulnerability and intended solution path
  • Exploit scripts or solve scripts (if applicable)
  • Expected output demonstrating a successful solve

The solve/ directory is for internal reference only — never expose it to players.

docs.title: Flag Injection
docs.text: Flags must be injected into containers via the FLAG environment variable at runtime. Never hardcode the flag in the image — hardcoded flags can be extracted by inspecting image layers.

docs.title: Standard pattern
docs.text:
Pass FLAG through docker-compose.yml:

environment:
  - FLAG=${FLAG}   # resolved from a .env file or Portainer secrets

Or when running manually:

docker run -e FLAG='CTF{your_flag}' -p 5000:5000 <challenge-name>

Inside the challenge code, read the flag from the environment:

Language Example Python os.environ.get("FLAG") Node.js process.env.FLAG Bash echo $FLAG C getenv("FLAG")

For compiled binaries or artifact-based challenges (forensics, stego, rev), use an entrypoint script to embed FLAG into the relevant file or location at container start — not at build time.

docs.title: The flag_env field
docs.text:
The flag_env field in challenge.yaml documents which environment variable the challenge reads the flag from. The default and expected value is FLAG.

Overriding flag_env is heavily discouraged. It exists only for the narrow edge case where a challenge wraps a third-party service that already expects a differently-named variable and cannot be modified. If you find yourself wanting to change it for any other reason, reconsider the challenge design instead.

docs.title: Local development
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the challenge directory (never commit it):

FLAG=CTF{test_flag_local}

Docker Compose resolves it automatically:

docker compose up --build

docs.title: Using Templates
docs.text:
The templates/ directory at the root of ctf-challenges/ contains a ready-to-copy scaffold for each category. To start a new challenge:

  1. Copy the relevant template:

bash cp -r templates/web 2026/web/easy/my-challenge

  1. Fill in challenge.yaml with the correct metadata.
  2. Add challenge source code to src/.
  3. Add the intended solution to solve/.
  4. Write the Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml for your stack.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Thing Convention Example Challenge directory lowercase-hyphen sqli-1, buffer-overflow-2 Docker image/service lowercase-hyphen web-sqli-1 challenge.yaml name lowercase-hyphen sqli-1 Flag format CTF{...} CTF{example_flag_here} Traefik router name lowercase-hyphen traefik.http.routers.sqli-1

docs.title: Port Guidelines
docs.text:
• All challenges use internal port 5000 by default
  • Avoid port conflicts between challenges running simultaneously on the same host
  • The internal port should always be 5000; the host port can vary

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
Before submitting a challenge:

  • [ ] docker build succeeds with no errors
  • [ ] Container starts and service is reachable on port 5000
  • [ ] Challenge behaves as intended
  • [ ] Flag is retrievable via the intended solution in solve/
  • [ ] No unnecessary services or open ports inside the container
  • [ ] challenge.yaml is fully filled out (including description)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Challenges must be self-contained — no external runtime dependencies
  • Avoid storing secrets in the image beyond the flag itself
  • Ensure reproducibility across environments

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This document describes the current manual deployment process for the CTF infrastructure and challenges.

Deployment is currently performed using Portainer and manual configuration of services.

docs.title: VPS Hardening (Initial Setup)
docs.text: This step is performed when provisioning a new VPS or rebuilding infrastructure. It is not required for every CTF deployment.

docs.title: 1. Secure SSH Access
docs.text:
• Disable password authentication
  • Enable key-based authentication
  • Disable root login
  • Optionally change default SSH port (non-essential, reduces noise)

docs.title: 2. Configure Firewall (UFW)
docs.text:
• Allow only required ports:
  • SSH
  • HTTP (80)
  • HTTPS (443)
  • Deny all other incoming traffic

docs.title: 3. Install and Configure Fail2Ban
docs.text:
• Protect SSH from brute-force attacks
  • Verify jail configuration is active

docs.title: 4. Enable Automatic Security Updates
docs.text:
• Configure unattended upgrades
  • Ensure system receives security patches regularly

docs.title: 5. Privilege Escalation Audit
docs.text:
• Run linpeas to identify potential privilege escalation vectors
  • Review and mitigate any critical findings

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to VPS (SSH)
  • Access to Portainer UI
  • All required Docker images built or available

docs.title: 1. Access Server
docs.text: • SSH into the VPS hosting the infrastructure

docs.title: 2. Open Portainer
docs.text:
• Navigate to Portainer web interface
  • Select the appropriate environment

docs.title: 3. Deploy / Update Core Infrastructure
docs.text:
• Create or update stack using Docker Compose (if applicable)
  • Ensure core services are running:
  • Traefik
  • Uptime Kuma

docs.title: 4. Deploy Challenge Containers
docs.text:
• Start individual challenge containers
  • Verify containers are running

docs.title: 5. Configure Routing (Traefik)
docs.text:
• Ensure each challenge is properly routed
  • Verify labels or routing rules

docs.title: 6. Verify Services
docs.text:
• Access each challenge via browser
  • Confirm correct responses

docs.title: 7. Monitor via Uptime Kuma
docs.text:
• Check that all services are marked “up”
  • Investigate any failures

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Traefik is running
  • [ ] All challenge containers are running
  • [ ] Routes are accessible
  • [ ] No 5xx errors
  • [ ] Uptime Kuma shows all services healthy

docs.title: Known Issues
docs.text:
• Traefik may not detect new containers immediately → restart may be required
  • Portainer UI can sometimes show stale state
  • Manual routing mistakes can break challenge access

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• Fully manual deployment
  • No automated validation of challenges
  • No rollback mechanism

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• VPS hardening should be completed before exposing services publicly
  • Security audits should be repeated periodically or after major infrastructure changes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide helps new members understand the CTF platform and get started with contributing.

docs.title: What You Need to Know
docs.text:
The platform consists of three main parts:

  • ctf-docs → documentation (you are here)
  • ctf-infra → infrastructure setup (Traefik, Docker, etc.)
  • ctf-challenges → challenge implementations

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
You should be familiar with:

  • Basic Linux commands
  • Git and GitHub workflow
  • Docker (basic usage)

docs.title: 1. Clone Repositories
docs.text:
git clone <ctf-docs>
git clone <ctf-challenges>
git clone <ctf-infra>
````

---

### 2. Install Required Tools

* Docker
* Python (for MkDocs if working on docs)
* Git

---

### 3. Run a Sample Challenge

Inside a challenge directory:

```bash
docker build -t test-challenge .
docker run -p 5000:5000 test-challenge

Then open:

http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Writing a Challenge
docs.text:
1. Follow the challenge structure standard
  2. Build and test locally
  3. Deploy using the runbook
  4. Verify everything works

docs.title: Working on Infrastructure
docs.text:
1. Understand current architecture
  2. Make changes carefully
  3. Test before deploying

docs.title: Improving Documentation
docs.text:
1. Update relevant docs
  2. Keep content clear and actionable
  3. Avoid unnecessary theory

docs.title: Recommended Reading
docs.text:
• Architecture Overview
  • Deployment Process
  • Add Challenge Runbook

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
• Keep things simple and reproducible
  • Document what you change
  • Test before pushing changes

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Ask in team group/chat
  • Check documentation first
  • Review existing challenges for reference

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This runbook describes the process of adding and deploying a new challenge to the CTF platform.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Challenge Docker image is built or Dockerfile is ready
  • Challenge runs correctly in local environment
  • Port and service details are known

docs.title: 1. Prepare Challenge Locally
docs.text:
• Build the Docker image: bash docker build -t challenge-name .

  • Run locally to verify:

bash docker run -p 5000:5000 challenge-name

  • Verify:

  • Service is accessible

  • Challenge works as intended
  • Flag is retrievable

docs.title: 2. Push Image (if using registry)
docs.text:
• Tag image:

bash docker tag challenge-name registry/challenge-name

  • Push to registry:

bash docker push registry/challenge-name

docs.title: 3. Deploy Container via Portainer
docs.text:
• Open Portainer UI

  • Create or start container:

  • Use image or build config

  • Assign port
  • Attach to correct Docker network

docs.title: 4. Configure Traefik Routing
docs.text:
• Add appropriate labels to container:

  • Enable Traefik

  • Define router rule (host/path)

  • Set service port

  • Example:

traefik.enable=true traefik.http.routers.challenge.rule=Host(`challenge.domain`)

docs.title: 5. Verify Deployment
docs.text:
• Access challenge via browser

  • Confirm:

  • Route works

  • No errors
  • Challenge behaves correctly

docs.title: 6. Add Monitoring
docs.text:
• Add endpoint to Uptime Kuma
  • Verify status is “up”

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Container is running
  • [ ] Challenge accessible via Traefik route
  • [ ] No errors in logs
  • [ ] Uptime Kuma shows healthy status

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
• Port mismatch between container and Traefik
  • Missing or incorrect labels
  • Container not attached to correct network
  • Service not listening on expected port

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Always test locally before deployment
  • Avoid port conflicts with existing challenges
  • Keep naming consistent across containers and routes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This runbook covers the end-to-end process for deploying all challenge containers for a CTF event — from pre-event preparation through event-day monitoring to post-event teardown.

docs.title: Pre-Event Checklist
docs.text: Complete these steps before the event starts.

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text:
• [ ] VPS is accessible via SSH
  • [ ] Traefik is running and healthy
  • [ ] Portainer is accessible
  • [ ] Uptime Kuma is running
  • [ ] DNS records for all challenge subdomains are configured and propagated

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
• [ ] All challenge Docker images build successfully (docker build)
  • [ ] All challenges run correctly in local testing (docker compose up)
  • [ ] Each challenge has a valid, fully-filled challenge.yaml (including description)
  • [ ] Flags are confirmed correct for all challenges
  • [ ] All docker-compose.yml files have correct Traefik labels and domain names

docs.title: 1. SSH into the VPS
docs.text: ssh <user>@<vps-ip>

docs.title: 2. Pull latest challenge code
docs.text: git pull

docs.title: 3. Build and start challenge containers
docs.text:
For each challenge, from its directory:

docker compose up --build -d

Or to deploy all challenges at once from the repo root (if using a top-level compose file):

docker compose up --build -d

docs.title: 4. Verify containers are running
docs.text:
docker ps

Confirm each challenge container appears with status Up.

docs.title: 5. Check Traefik routing
docs.text:
1. Open the Traefik dashboard (if enabled)
  2. Confirm each challenge router appears under HTTP Routers
  3. Verify the Host(...) rule matches the intended subdomain

If a challenge does not appear in Traefik:

# Restart Traefik to force label re-discovery
docker restart traefik

docs.title: 6. Verify challenge accessibility
docs.text:
For each challenge, confirm it is reachable via its public URL:

curl -I https://<challenge-name>.<domain>

Expected: 200 OK (or appropriate response for the challenge type).

docs.title: 7. Register endpoints in Uptime Kuma
docs.text:
For each newly deployed challenge:

  1. Open Uptime Kuma
  2. Add a new monitor:
  3. Type: HTTP(s) or TCP depending on challenge type
  4. URL / Address: the challenge's public URL or host:port
  5. Friendly Name: challenge name
  6. Confirm the monitor shows Up within the check interval

docs.title: Event-Day Monitoring
docs.text:
During the event, monitor the following:

  • [ ] Uptime Kuma dashboard — all challenges show Up
  • [ ] Container logs for errors: bash docker logs <container-id> --follow
  • [ ] Traefik logs for routing errors: bash docker logs traefik --follow
  • [ ] VPS resource usage (CPU, memory, disk): bash htop df -h

docs.title: Restarting a broken challenge
docs.text:
docker compose restart <service-name>

Or force a full rebuild if the image is suspect:

docker compose up --build -d <service-name>

docs.title: Known Issues
docs.text:
• Traefik does not detect new containers immediately — restart Traefik if a challenge route is missing after deployment.
  • Portainer UI may show stale state — refresh or restart Portainer if container status appears incorrect.
  • Port conflicts — if a challenge fails to start, check that its host port is not already in use: docker ps or ss -tlnp.

docs.title: Post-Event Teardown
docs.text: After the event has concluded:

docs.title: 1. Stop and remove challenge containers
docs.text: docker compose down

docs.title: 2. Remove challenge images (optional, to free disk space)
docs.text: docker image prune -a

docs.title: 3. Disable challenge monitors in Uptime Kuma
docs.text: Mark each challenge monitor as paused or delete it.

docs.title: 4. Archive challenge containers
docs.text: Confirm all challenge source code, flags, and solve scripts are committed to ctf-challenges before removing anything locally.

docs.title: Verification Checklist (Post-Deployment)
docs.text:
• [ ] All challenge containers are running (docker ps)
  • [ ] All challenge URLs return expected responses
  • [ ] Traefik routes are correctly configured
  • [ ] No 5xx errors in Traefik logs
  • [ ] Uptime Kuma shows all services healthy
  • [ ] No unnecessary ports are exposed on the VPS
